text: The design and additive manufacturing of metamaterials with tailored architectures allow for the realization of unprecedented mechanical properties that surpass those of their individual constituents. Traditionally, these properties are characterized by response curves, such as the stress-strain profiles that define a material’s quasi-static signature. Despite recent progress, current inverse design frameworks struggle to replicate specific, complex behaviors due to the challenges of multi-objective optimization, inherent non-linearity, and the prevalence of process-dependent manufacturing defects. In this study, we present an accelerated inverse design methodology that integrates generative machine learning with desktop additive manufacturing to synthesize nearly any uniaxial compressive stress-strain profile while accounting for structural deviations introduced during printing. Our findings demonstrate that full mechanical tailorability is achievable, maintaining nearly 90% fidelity between predicted targets and experimental measurements. By streamlining the path from prescribed behavior to physical realization, this approach provides a robust framework for engineering advanced materials while bypassing traditional, time-consuming iterative design-manufacturing cycles.
